1: 'This is what the Lord GOD showed me: behold, he was forming locusts when the latter growth was just beginning to sprout, and behold, it was the latter growth after the king's mowings.' The first of five visions (Amos 7:1-3, Amos 7:4-6, Amos 7:7-9, Amos 8:1-3, Amos 9:1). 'The latter growth after the king's mowings' (hineh leqesh achar gizze hamelech) refers to the agricultural practice: the first mowing of the spring crop went to the king as royal taxation (compare 1 Samuel 8:15: 'he will take one-tenth of your grain'). The 'latter growth' (leqesh) was the smaller second crop that the farmer kept. YHWH forming locusts (goze arbeh) to destroy this second crop — the farmer's share after the king took his — would mean total ruin for ordinary people.
2: 'When the locusts had finished eating the vegetation of the land, I said, O Lord GOD, please forgive! How can Jacob stand? He is so small!' Amos intercedes — perhaps unexpectedly for a prophet of judgment. 'How can Jacob stand? He is so small' (mi yaqum Ya'aqov ki qaton hu) — the argument from weakness, not from merit. This is similar to the Mosaic intercession pattern (Exodus 32:11-14, Numbers 14:13-19) where the prophet argues not for Israel's righteousness but for divine mercy and YHWH's own purpose. 'Jacob' as the name for the nation — the patriarch who himself was small and dependent on divine provision.
3: 'The LORD relented concerning this: It shall not be, said the LORD.' YHWH 'repented' (nacham) of this judgment — the same verb used in Genesis 6:6-7 (YHWH regretting making humanity) and Exodus 32:14 (YHWH relenting from destroying Israel after the golden calf). The theological paradox of prophetic intercession: the predetermined judgment is modified by prophetic prayer. The first two visions both end with divine relenting in response to intercession — establishing that Amos's intercession was effective. The third vision (Amos 7:7-9) notably does not include intercession and does not end with relenting.
4: 'This is what the Lord GOD showed me: behold, the Lord GOD was calling for a judgment by fire, and it devoured the great deep and was eating up the land.' The second vision: fire of judgment devouring the 'great deep' (tehom rabbah) — the subterranean water source that feeds springs and wells (cf. Genesis 7:11, Amos 49:25). A fire that consumes the deep would dry up all underground water, creating comprehensive drought. The vision escalates from locusts (destroying the harvest) to fire (destroying the water supply itself), building the case that YHWH has multiple instruments of comprehensive judgment available.
5: 'Then I said, O Lord GOD, please cease! How can Jacob stand? He is so small!' The second intercession uses the same form as the first — the repetition is liturgically deliberate, like a pattern of prayer. The parallel structure (vision → divine action → prophetic intercession → divine relenting) establishes Amos's intercessory role. Unlike the writing prophets who are often presented primarily as proclaimers of judgment, Amos here shows the intercessory dimension — standing between YHWH and the people to plead, as Moses did (Exodus 32:11, Deuteronomy 9:18-20).
6: 'The LORD relented concerning this: This also shall not be, said the Lord GOD.' The second relenting follows the same pattern. 'This also shall not be' — the emphatic 'also' marks the continuation of the intercessory success. But the three-part structure (locusts, fire, plumb line) follows the pattern of warning escalation: the first two warnings are rescinded; the third is definitive. This narrative structure teaches that prophetic intercession is effective but not unlimited — there is a point of no return that the third vision reaches.
7: 'This is what he showed me: behold, the Lord was standing beside a wall built with a plumb line, with a plumb line in his hand.' The plumb line (anakh) is a weight on a string used by builders to verify that a wall is perfectly vertical — a standard construction tool in the ancient world. Stone walls built without a plumb line are vulnerable to collapse; a wall 'made with a plumb line' is one that was built correctly. YHWH standing beside such a wall with a plumb line is the image of a building inspector about to test whether the structure meets its original specification — i.e., whether Israel meets the covenant standard.
8: 'And the LORD said to me, Amos, what do you see? And I said, A plumb line. Then the Lord said, Behold, I am setting a plumb line in the midst of my people Israel; I will never again pass by them.' The direct dialogue between YHWH and prophet is characteristic of the vision reports. 'I will never again pass by them' (lo osif od avor lo) — the decisive break from the first two visions: no more relenting, no more passing over judgment. The phrase 'pass by' (avor) may echo the Passover/Exodus language (YHWH 'passing by' to judge Egypt, Exodus 12:12-13) — now inverted: YHWH will not pass by Israel without judgment.
9: 'And the high places of Isaac shall be made desolate, and the sanctuaries of Israel shall be laid waste, and I will rise against the house of Jeroboam with the sword.' 'High places of Isaac' (bamot Yitzchaq) — the local worship sites associated with the patriarchal tradition (cf. Genesis 26:24-25: Isaac building an altar at Beersheba). 'Sanctuaries of Israel' (miqdeshei Yisrael) — the royal cult centers including Bethel and Dan. The specific mention of Jeroboam's house: the word of YHWH through Amos against Jeroboam II reaches the ears of Amaziah the priest (v10), who reports it as sedition. This was not mere religious controversy but a political threat claim.
10: 'Then Amaziah the priest of Bethel sent to Jeroboam king of Israel, saying, Amos has conspired against you in the midst of the house of Israel. The land is not able to bear all his words.' The Amaziah confrontation is the only extended narrative in Amos. Amaziah was the 'priest of Bethel' (kohen Bet-El) — the head of the royal cult center established by Jeroboam I (1 Kings 12:29-33). His report to King Jeroboam II frames Amos's prophecy as political conspiracy (qesher) — the term used for treasonous palace coups (2 Kings 15:10-15). In the ANE, a prophet who predicted the death of a king was legally dangerous — Jeremiah 26:11 shows Jeremiah later facing death for similar prophecies.
11: 'For thus Amos has said, Jeroboam shall die by the sword, and Israel must go into exile away from his land.' Amaziah paraphrases Amos's message accurately (cf. Amos 7:9), and the political charge is clear: predicting the king's violent death was tantamount to planning it in ancient Near Eastern legal thinking. Kings routinely consulted prophets for legitimation; a prophet who predicted their downfall was functionally an enemy of the state. The historical record: Jeroboam II did not die by the sword (2 Kings 14:29 records he 'slept with his fathers'), though his son Zechariah was assassinated after only six months (2 Kings 15:10).
12: 'And Amaziah said to Amos, O seer, go, flee away to the land of Judah, and eat bread there, and prophesy there.' 'Seer' (chozeh) rather than 'prophet' (nabhi) — the distinction may be dismissive or technical. Amaziah's command to 'eat bread' in Judah is a reference to prophetic income: professional prophets received payment (cf. 1 Samuel 9:7-8: paying Saul a fee before consulting Samuel). Amaziah assumes Amos is a professional prophet who has come to Bethel for income and can be sent back to his own territory. This misunderstanding provides the occasion for Amos's most famous self-identification.
13: 'But never again prophesy at Bethel, for it is the king's sanctuary, and it is a temple of the kingdom.' 'The king's sanctuary' (miqdash-melek) and 'temple of the kingdom' (beit mamlakhah) — Amaziah reveals Bethel's actual status: it is a royal chapel, not an independent religious institution. The Bethel sanctuary was founded specifically to prevent the northern kingdom's subjects from going to Jerusalem (1 Kings 12:27-28); it was architecturally and institutionally linked to royal power. Amaziah's authority to expel Amos derives from the sanctuary's royal patronage, not from religious principle.
14: 'Then Amos answered and said to Amaziah, I was no prophet, nor a prophet's son, but I was a herdsman and a dresser of sycamore figs.' One of the most analyzed statements in prophetic literature. 'I was no prophet' (lo nabhi anochi) — the form could be past tense (I was not a prophet before my calling) or present (I am not a prophet in the professional sense). 'A prophet's son' (ben-nabhi) refers to the prophetic guilds (benei hanevi'im) — schools of prophets associated with Samuel (1 Samuel 10:10), Elijah (2 Kings 2:3), and Elisha (2 Kings 4:38). A noqed (herdsman/sheep-breeder) — the same term used of Mesha king of Moab in 2 Kings 3:4. Sycamore fig-dresser (boles shiqmim) — sycamore figs require a specific treatment (pricking or scoring) to ripen properly.
15: 'But the LORD took me from following the flock, and the LORD said to me, Go, prophesy to my people Israel.' The direct divine call without mediation — 'the LORD took me' (wayyiqacheni YHWH) from the flock. This parallels David called from shepherding (2 Samuel 7:8: 'I took you from the pasture, from following the sheep'), Moses called from shepherding (Exodus 3:1-4), and the general OT pattern of divine call as social disruption. The call is non-institutional: no prophetic school, no mentor-figure, no royal appointment. The prophetic authority derives entirely from the divine commission, not from institutional standing.
16: 'Now therefore hear the word of the LORD. You say, Do not prophesy against Israel, and do not preach against the house of Isaac.' Amaziah's prohibition ('do not prophesy against Israel') is turned back on Amaziah himself — he is now the subject of a personal oracle. The phrase 'the house of Isaac' (beit Yitzchaq) echoes the 'high places of Isaac' (Amos 7:9), using the patriarchal name for the northern kingdom. The authority claimed by Amaziah to silence prophetic speech is itself the subject of prophetic judgment: the silencer of the word will hear the word pronounced against him personally.
17: 'Therefore thus says the LORD: Your wife shall be a prostitute in the city, and your sons and daughters shall fall by the sword, and your land shall be divided up with a measuring line; you yourself shall die in an unclean land, and Israel shall surely go into exile away from its land.' The oracle against Amaziah is comprehensive: his wife, children, land, and his own death in a foreign (unclean) land. 'A prostitute in the city' — the seizure of a defeated city's women as spoils (cf. Isaiah 13:16, Zechariah 14:2). 'Your land shall be divided with a measuring line' — the Assyrian practice of redistributing conquered territory to settlers. 'Die in an unclean land' — dying outside the covenant land meant burial without covenant community, a profound curse.
